output:
Using the skill to produce the full audit now. Running all 10 sections against the provided context.

---

# LegalSum — Launch Readiness Audit
**Product:** B2B SaaS for Legal Teams | **ICP:** Mid-size law firms | **Stage:** Ready to Launch
**Date:** 2026-03-06

---

## Pre-Audit Economics Snapshot

```
AI cost per user/month:  50 requests × $0.03 = $1.50
Monthly revenue/user:    $49.00
AI cost as % of revenue: 3.06%   ← well under 30% threshold
Gross margin (AI-adj):   96.9%   ← excellent for AI-first SaaS
```

---

## Section 1 — Pre-Launch Validation

[FAIL] 50+ customer discovery calls completed
  → Next step: Before launch, document that you've conducted structured discovery interviews with at least 50 attorneys or legal ops leads at mid-size firms. If you haven't, run 10 more this week using a screener focused on document volume and current summarization workflow pain.

[FAIL] Pre-payment secured from 3+ customers
  → Next step: Convert at least 3 prospects to paid before public launch. Offer a 3-month founder rate ($29/month) in exchange for payment upfront and a testimonial commitment.

[FAIL] Problem validated beyond interest ("sounds cool")
  → Next step: Confirm you have written evidence (email, call notes, LOI) that at least 3 firms have an active workflow where summarizing documents costs them measurable time (e.g., "2 hours per matter"). Interest is not validation — willingness to pay is.

[FAIL] Manual service delivery tested before automation
  → Next step: Verify you ran the summarization workflow manually for at least one paying client before automating it. This confirms the output meets legal-grade expectations and informs prompt engineering for accuracy and tone.

---

## Section 2 — Pricing Strategy

[FAIL] Pricing model is outcome/usage-based or hybrid — NOT flat rate only
  → Next step: $49/month flat rate is a per-seat model in disguise. Law firms vary enormously in document volume. A firm doing 500 matters/month gets the same price as one doing 5. Introduce a usage tier: e.g., $49/month for up to 100 summaries, $99/month for up to 300, $199/month for unlimited. This protects your margin at scale and aligns price to value delivered.

[FAIL] Value metric clearly identified and tied to pricing
  → Next step: Define your value metric explicitly. For LegalSum, it is likely "matters summarized" or "documents processed." Every pricing tier should reference this metric so buyers understand what they're paying for.

[FAIL] 3–4 pricing tiers with clear differentiation
  → Next step: A single $49/month plan gives enterprise prospects no upgrade path and signals low seriousness to legal procurement teams. Build at minimum: Solo ($29, up to 50 docs), Team ($49, up to 150 docs, 3 seats), Firm ($149, up to 500 docs, unlimited seats + DPA). Add an Enterprise tier on request.

[FAIL] Annual contract option with discount configured
  → Next step: Add an annual billing option at 20% discount (e.g., $39/month billed annually). Law firms often have annual software budgets and will prefer annual contracts — this also improves your cash flow.

[FAIL] Pricing page live and scannable in under 5 seconds
  → Next step: URL is a placeholder. Ensure a live pricing page exists at /pricing with tier names, prices, included volume, and a feature comparison table before launch.

---

## Section 3 — Payment Integration

[FAIL] Stripe account activated with live (not test) keys
  → Next step: Confirm your Stripe account has completed identity verification and is in live mode. Test mode charges never hit your bank account. Check Stripe Dashboard → Settings → Account Details.

[FAIL] End-to-end test transactions successful
  → Next step: Complete a full purchase flow using a real card (Stripe's $1 test charge trick: charge $1, then refund). Verify receipt emails, webhook delivery, and subscription creation all fire correctly.

[FAIL] Webhook endpoints configured and tested
  → Next step: Configure Stripe webhooks for at minimum: `checkout.session.completed`, `customer.subscription.deleted`, `invoice.payment_failed`. Use Stripe CLI (`stripe listen --forward-to localhost:3000/webhooks`) to test locally before going live.

[FAIL] Customer billing portal accessible
  → Next step: Enable Stripe's Customer Portal so users can self-serve plan changes and cancellations. Link it from your app's account settings page. This is legally required in some jurisdictions and reduces churn-by-frustration.

[FAIL] Tax and KYC compliance requirements met
  → Next step: Enable Stripe Tax for automatic sales tax calculation across US states. For international sales to law firms in the EU, verify VAT obligations. Law firms are sophisticated buyers who will notice tax errors on invoices.

---

## Section 4 — AI Economics

[PASS] Inference cost calculated per action
  Cost: $0.03/request (GPT-4 document summarization). Confirmed and documented.

[PASS] AI costs under 30% of revenue
  $1.50 / $49 = 3.06%. Significant headroom. Even at 10× current usage, margin remains healthy.

[PASS] Gross margin exceeds 50% (AI-adjusted)
  ~96.9% gross margin. Excellent for AI-first SaaS. Industry benchmark for AI-first: 50–65%. You exceed traditional SaaS targets.

[FAIL] Margin protection strategy exists for high-volume users
  → Next step: A single user doing 500 requests/month costs you $15 in inference — still only 30.6% of revenue. But define a cap or overage policy now, before a law firm with 200 associates starts hammering the API. Set a soft cap with usage alerts and an overage fee ($0.02–0.05/request above tier limit) before launch.

[FAIL] Cost monitoring and alerts configured
  → Next step: Set up an OpenAI spend alert at $500/month (or 80% of your projected monthly AI budget). Configure this in the OpenAI usage dashboard under Billing → Limits. Add application-level logging so you can see per-user inference cost in your own analytics.

---

## Section 5 — GEO & AI Discovery

[FAIL] AI visibility audit completed (ChatGPT, Perplexity, Claude)
  → Next step: Before launch, query "AI document summarization for law firms" and "legal document summarization tools" in ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Claude. Note which competitors appear. If LegalSum is not mentioned, you are invisible to the ~33% of discovery traffic now routed through AI assistants. This is a baseline you need to establish at launch, not 6 months later.

[FAIL] Structured data and FAQ schema implemented
  → Next step: Add FAQ schema (JSON-LD) to your pricing and homepage answering questions like "How does LegalSum handle attorney-client privilege?" and "Is LegalSum SOC 2 certified?" These are the exact questions AI assistants will surface to legal buyers.

[FAIL] Content uses authoritative, citation-worthy language
  → Next step: Review homepage and product copy. Replace hedged language ("might help," "could save time") with direct claims backed by specifics ("Summarizes a 40-page deposition in under 30 seconds"). AI models cite confident, specific content over vague marketing copy.

[FAIL] SSR or pre-rendering confirmed for LLM crawler compatibility
  → Next step: Confirm your app uses server-side rendering or static generation (Next.js, Nuxt, or similar). Single-page apps that require JavaScript execution are partially or fully invisible to LLM crawlers. Verify with `curl -s https://legalsum.example.com | grep -i "legal"` — if your content appears in the raw HTML, you're fine.

[FAIL] Server response time under 200ms
  → Next step: Run a Lighthouse audit or use WebPageTest.org post-launch to confirm TTFB is under 200ms. Deploy to a CDN edge location close to US legal markets (East Coast, specifically NY and DC).

[FAIL] Core Web Vitals passing (LCP, CLS, FID)
  → Next step: After launch, submit to Google Search Console and run PageSpeed Insights. Target LCP < 2.5s, CLS < 0.1, FID < 100ms. These affect both traditional SEO and AI crawler signal quality.

---

## Section 6 — Competitor Intelligence

[FAIL] 3+ direct competitors identified and documented
  → Next step: Document at minimum: Harvey AI, Casetext (now Thomson Reuters), and Luminance. There are also vertical-agnostic tools like Glean and Notion AI being used in legal teams. You need to know their positioning before a prospect asks "how are you different from Harvey?"

[FAIL] Competitor pricing documented
  → Next step: Research and record each competitor's pricing model, entry price, and target firm size. Harvey targets BigLaw (not your ICP) — that's a differentiator. Casetext was $150+/user/month before acquisition. Knowing this makes your $49 flat rate a concrete advantage in mid-market positioning.

[FAIL] Written differentiation statement (one sentence)
  → Next step: Write and publish a single sentence that completes: "LegalSum is the only tool that ___." Example: "LegalSum is the only document summarization tool built exclusively for mid-size law firms that processes matters in bulk without requiring IT setup." If you can't write this sentence, pause launch and clarify your positioning first.

[FAIL] AI visibility comparison vs. competitors completed
  → Next step: Query "best document summarization tools for law firms" in 3 AI assistants and document which tools they recommend. This is your baseline competitive AI visibility score. If competitors appear and you don't, create comparison content (e.g., "LegalSum vs. Harvey: Mid-Market Alternative") before launch.

[FAIL] Citation sentiment analyzed for competitor mentions
  → Next step: Review how AI assistants describe each competitor — are they presented as enterprise-only, expensive, or complex? If yes, position LegalSum explicitly as the accessible mid-market alternative in all public-facing content.

---

## Section 7 — Social Media & Launch Prep

[FAIL] Primary platform profiles created (LinkedIn priority for B2B)
  → Next step: For B2B legal SaaS, create and fully complete: LinkedIn company page (most critical), X/Twitter account. Secondary: a Product Hunt profile. Avoid spreading thin — LinkedIn and PH are your launch-week channels.

[FAIL] LinkedIn founder profile optimized for legal SaaS buyer discovery
  → Next step: Update your LinkedIn headline to reference legal tech specifically (e.g., "Building AI document summarization for law firms | Founder @ LegalSum"). Add a featured post linking to LegalSum. Attorneys evaluate founders before buying software — your credibility here is part of the product.

[FAIL] X/Twitter account established with pre-launch content
  → Next step: Create the account and post 5–10 pieces of content before launch week: behind-the-scenes building posts, "did you know" stats about legal document review time, and a pinned post about LegalSum. Accounts with no history look abandoned.

[FAIL] Product Hunt profile prepared and scheduled
  → Next step: Create your PH maker profile and draft your product listing (tagline, description, gallery images, first comment). Schedule launch for a Tuesday or Wednesday, 12:01 AM PT. Do not go live on a Monday or Friday.

[FAIL] 400+ Product Hunt supporters contacted in advance
  → Next step: Start outreach now. DM past customers, LinkedIn connections in legal/tech, and fellow founders. Ask them to be notified of your PH launch and to support with a comment — not just an upvote. Comments drive algorithmic ranking more than upvotes alone.

[FAIL] Pre-launch email sequence drafted
  → Next step: Write a 5-email launch sequence: (1) "we're launching soon," (2) "here's the problem we solve," (3) "launch day — we're live," (4) "day 3 follow-up with social proof," (5) "last chance for launch pricing." Schedule in your email platform before launch week.

[FAIL] 30-day post-launch content calendar planned
  → Next step: Map out 30 days of LinkedIn content: customer win stories, feature walkthroughs, legal industry stats, behind-the-scenes. Commit to 3 posts/week minimum. Law firm buyers do research over 2–6 weeks — consistent presence matters more than launch day volume.

[FAIL] "Magic Triangle" operational (founder content + outreach + retargeting)
  → Next step: Set up the three legs before launch: (1) founder publishing on LinkedIn 3×/week, (2) direct outreach to 20 ICP prospects/week, (3) retargeting pixel on your site to capture visitors from launch traffic. Without retargeting, launch-week visitors are lost forever.

---

## Section 8 — Legal & Compliance

**Note: This section is CRITICAL for your ICP. Law firms are among the most legally sophisticated buyers. Missing compliance items will kill deals in procurement review.**

[FAIL] Privacy Policy live at /privacy
  → Next step: Publish a Privacy Policy that explicitly addresses: what document content is sent to OpenAI, whether it is used for training (critical — law firms will ask), data retention periods, and right to deletion. The fact that client documents may contain privileged information makes this a deal-breaker if missing.

[FAIL] Terms of Service live at /terms
  → Next step: Publish Terms that include: acceptable use policy, disclaimer of liability for AI output errors (lawyers will hold you to every word of a summary — limit liability explicitly), IP ownership of processed documents, and termination clauses. Have a lawyer review these, especially liability language.

[FAIL] Cookie consent banner configured
  → Next step: Install a GDPR/CCPA-compliant cookie banner (Cookiebot, CookieYes, or similar). Law firms with EU clients or California presence will audit this in vendor reviews.

[FAIL] Data Processing Agreement (DPA) template ready for B2B clients
  → Next step: This is non-negotiable for law firm sales. Law firms are data controllers; you are a data processor. You need a signed DPA before they will send you client documents. Draft a standard DPA template (use a legal template service or an attorney). Also obtain and publish OpenAI's DPA so firms can see the processor chain. Without this, enterprise procurement will stall every deal.

**Additional legal risk item specific to your product:**

[FAIL] AI accuracy disclaimer and attorney review requirement documented
  → Next step: Add explicit language in your ToS and in-app UI that LegalSum summaries are for attorney review only and do not constitute legal advice. A missed fact in a summary that causes a missed deadline or filing error creates liability exposure. Disclaim this clearly and consider E&O insurance before launch.

---

## Section 9 — Trust Infrastructure

**Note: Legal teams have higher trust requirements than typical SaaS buyers. Audit logs and data isolation are baseline expectations, not differentiators.**

[FAIL] Authentication system is production-ready with MFA
  → Next step: Confirm your auth provider (Auth0, Clerk, Supabase Auth, etc.) is configured with MFA enforced for all accounts. Law firms will require MFA as a condition of use. Verify password reset flows and session timeout (recommend 8-hour session max for legal contexts).

[FAIL] Audit logging enabled for all document processing events
  → Next step: Log every document upload, summarization request, and result delivery with timestamps and user IDs. Law firms need audit trails for compliance. Store logs in append-only storage (S3 with Object Lock, or similar). This is a key trust differentiator — expose a log viewer in the app.

[FAIL] Error tracking configured (Sentry or equivalent)
  → Next step: Install Sentry with source map uploads and alert on all unhandled exceptions. A law firm discovering a silent error in their summary pipeline will churn immediately and tell colleagues.

[FAIL] Uptime monitoring and status page active
  → Next step: Set up Better Uptime, UptimeRobot, or Checkly with 1-minute interval monitoring. Publish a public status page (e.g., status.legalsum.example.com). Law firms expect enterprise-grade reliability transparency — a status page signals this before you have the SLA to back it up.

[FAIL] Security headers configured (HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options)
  → Next step: Run your domain through securityheaders.com post-launch. Configure HSTS, Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, and X-Content-Type-Options at minimum. Legal IT security reviews will check these. Target an A or A+ rating.

---

## Section 10 — Launch Day Readiness

[FAIL] Support channel operational and monitored
  → Next step: Set up a support email (support@legalsum.example.com) with a response SLA of 4 business hours for launch week. Consider Intercom or Crisp for in-app chat — law firms asking pre-sales questions during trial will expect near-real-time response. Unresponsive support = instant churn for legal buyers.

[FAIL] FAQ / Knowledge base published
  → Next step: Publish at minimum 10 FAQ articles before launch covering: how documents are processed, whether data is used for AI training, security and compliance, how to upload documents, and what output formats are supported. The most common question from legal buyers: "Does OpenAI train on our client documents?" Answer this prominently.

[FAIL] Analytics tracking verified (event-level, not just pageviews)
  → Next step: Confirm PostHog, Mixpanel, or Amplitude is capturing: signup, first document upload, first summary generated, subscription started, and cancellation. Pageview analytics alone will not tell you where users drop off in the activation funnel.

[FAIL] Team roles assigned for launch week
  → Next step: Assign explicit ownership before launch day: who monitors PH comments (respond within 15 minutes on launch day), who handles support tickets, who watches error logs, and who has authority to push a hotfix. If you're a solo founder, document your own triage order.

[FAIL] Rollback plan documented
  → Next step: Write a one-page runbook: what constitutes a P0 incident (e.g., summaries returning blank output, payment failures), how to revert to a previous deployment, and how to communicate downtime to active users. For legal teams, a silent failure is worse than a transparent outage.

---

## Audit Summary

```
Section                     PASS    FAIL
─────────────────────────────────────────
1. Pre-Launch Validation       0       4
2. Pricing Strategy            0       5
3. Payment Integration         0       5
4. AI Economics                3       2
5. GEO & AI Discovery          0       6
6. Competitor Intelligence     0       5
7. Social Media & Launch       0       8
8. Legal & Compliance          0       5  ← CRITICAL RISK
9. Trust Infrastructure        0       5  ← HIGH RISK
10. Launch Day Readiness       0       5
─────────────────────────────────────────
TOTAL                          3      50
```

**Overall Status: NOT LAUNCH-READY**

---

## Prioritized Fix Order

The 3 PASSes (AI unit economics) are your biggest strength — your margin profile is exceptional. Everything else needs work before public launch. Address in this order:

**Week 1 — Unblock Sales (Legal blockers that will kill every deal)**
1. Draft Privacy Policy with explicit OpenAI data handling disclosure
2. Draft Terms of Service with AI liability disclaimer
3. Draft DPA template for client contracts
4. Add in-app "AI accuracy / attorney review required" disclaimer

**Week 2 — Fix Pricing**
5. Replace flat rate with 3-tier usage-based pricing
6. Add annual billing option
7. Publish live pricing page with clear tier comparison

**Week 3 — Infrastructure**
8. Verify Stripe live mode, webhooks, and billing portal
9. Enable audit logging and surface it in-app
10. Set up uptime monitoring and status page
11. Implement MFA on auth

**Week 4 — Launch Prep**
12. Publish Privacy, ToS, DPA, and FAQ pages
13. Set up LinkedIn, X, and Product Hunt profiles
14. Begin PH supporter outreach (needs 3–4 weeks minimum)
15. Draft launch email sequence
16. Configure Sentry and event analytics

**Launch Week**
17. Security headers and Core Web Vitals check
18. Pre-launch AI visibility baseline (ChatGPT/Perplexity query)
19. Team runbook and rollback plan documented
20. Support channel live and monitored

---

*Re-run this audit after completing Week 1–2 fixes. The legal compliance gaps (Section 8) are the only items that will actively prevent closed deals — all other failures slow growth but don't block it.*

assertions.text: The audit output uses explicit [PASS] and [FAIL] labels (or equivalent PASS/FAIL markers) for individual checklist items, not vague recommendations or prose summaries.
assertions.reasoning: The output consistently uses explicit [PASS] and [FAIL] labels throughout all sections visible in the output.

assertions.text: The report states a specific threshold that AI inference costs should be less than 30% of revenue.
assertions.reasoning: The report explicitly states the 30% threshold: 'AI costs under 30% of revenue' and calculates '3.06% ← well under 30% threshold'.

assertions.text: The report distinguishes between traditional SaaS gross margin targets (80-90%) and AI-first SaaS targets (50-65%).
assertions.reasoning: The report mentions 'Industry benchmark for AI-first: 50–65%' but does not state the traditional SaaS gross margin target of 80–90% for comparison.

assertions.text: The report references the 2026 statistic that AI agents account for approximately 33% of organic search activity, or that only 12% of companies appear across all major AI platforms.
assertions.reasoning: The output is truncated and the GEO section references AI discovery traffic (~33%) but does not cite the 2026 statistic about 33% of organic search or the 12% of companies appearing across all major AI platforms.

assertions.text: The social/launch section recommends LinkedIn, Product Hunt, and G2 or Capterra specifically as primary platforms for a B2B SaaS product.
assertions.reasoning: The output is truncated before reaching the social/launch section, so there is no visible recommendation of LinkedIn, Product Hunt, and G2 or Capterra.

assertions.text: The pricing section flags the flat-rate model as a concern and recommends outcome-based, usage-based, or hybrid pricing, noting that per-seat pricing is declining.
assertions.reasoning: The pricing section explicitly flags the flat-rate model as 'a per-seat model in disguise' and recommends usage-based tiers, though it does not explicitly state 'per-seat pricing is declining'.
